stem: What is the legal difference between 'stopping' (stilstaan) and 'parking' (parkeren)?

choices.text: There is no legal difference
Stopping is a brief halt to let someone in or out or to unload immediately; longer halts or leaving the vehicle counts as parking
Stopping is only allowed at red lights
Parking is only allowed at meters; stopping is allowed anywhere

explanation.short: Stopping is a brief halt for immediate boarding or unloading; anything longer is parking.
explanation.examTip: Some places forbid stopping; others forbid only parking — read the sign.

stem: A continuous yellow line along the kerb means:

choices.text: Parking is forbidden, but you may stop briefly
Stopping is forbidden, not even to let a passenger out
Paid parking applies
Reserved for residents

explanation.short: A continuous yellow kerb line forbids stopping entirely.
explanation.examTip: A broken yellow line forbids only parking; brief stops are still allowed.

stem: How close to a junction may you park?

choices.text: Up to the corner itself
No closer than 5 metres
No closer than 12 metres
No closer than 25 metres

explanation.short: You may not park within 5 metres of a junction.
explanation.examTip: 12 metres is the rule for bus stops, not junctions.

image.alt: Plan view of a T-junction with a five-metre no-parking zone marked at the corner; a legally parked car sits beyond the five-metre limit.
image.caption: Keep at least 5 metres clear of a junction

stem: How far from a bus stop sign must you stay clear when parking?

choices.text: 5 metres
10 metres
12 metres
25 metres

explanation.short: You must stay at least 12 metres clear of a bus stop sign.
explanation.examTip: This ensures the bus can pull in and out safely.

stem: How close to a pedestrian crossing may you stop?

choices.text: Right at the crossing line
Not within 5 metres before it
Not within 10 metres before it
Stopping there is always permitted

explanation.short: You may not stop within 5 metres before a pedestrian crossing.
explanation.examTip: This rule preserves the sightline for both drivers and pedestrians.

image.alt: A zebra pedestrian crossing with a five-metre no-stopping zone marked before it; a car must stop no closer than 5 metres ahead of the crossing.
image.caption: Do not stop within 5 metres before a pedestrian crossing

stem: What does a blue line along the kerb indicate?

choices.text: Bus lane
Disabled-only parking
Blue zone — display a parking disc set to your arrival time
No stopping at any time

explanation.short: A blue line marks a blue zone where a parking disc must be displayed.
explanation.examTip: Round the arrival time up to the next half-hour on the disc.

stem: A sign reads 'vergunninghouders'. As a visitor without a permit, may you park there?

choices.text: Yes, for up to 1 hour
Yes, if you pay at a nearby meter
No, the space is reserved for permit holders
Yes, on Sundays only

explanation.short: A 'vergunninghouders' sign reserves the space for permit holders only.
explanation.examTip: A combined sign with 'betaald' lets visitors pay; permit holders still park free.

stem: A bay shows the wheelchair symbol with no number plate on the sign. Who may use it?

choices.text: Any vehicle, briefly
Only the specific permit holder whose plate is shown
Any holder of a valid Dutch or European disabled parking card displaying it
Electric vehicles only

explanation.short: A general disabled bay is open to any valid cardholder displaying the card.
explanation.examTip: A bay with a specific plate on the sign is reserved for that one permit only.

stem: You park an electric car in a bay marked 'opladen'. Once charging is finished you should:

choices.text: Leave the car there for the rest of the day
Move the car so others can charge
Disconnect the cable but leave the car
Stay only if a permit is displayed

explanation.short: Charging bays are for vehicles that are actively charging; move on when done.
explanation.examTip: Leaving a non-charging EV blocks the space and is fineable.

stem: A bay is signed 'laden en lossen'. You may park there to:

choices.text: Run a quick errand in a shop
Actively load or unload goods, staying with the vehicle
Wait for a passenger for up to 30 minutes
Charge an electric vehicle

explanation.short: A loading bay is for active loading or unloading only.
explanation.examTip: You must remain with the vehicle or be visibly handling goods.

stem: On a normal two-way street, how must you park your car?

choices.text: Facing oncoming traffic on the left kerb
Facing the direction of travel on your side of the road
Either direction, on either side
Only on the side with even house numbers

explanation.short: You must park facing the direction of travel on your own side of the road.
explanation.examTip: On a one-way street you may park on either side, still facing forward.

stem: Before opening your door after parking, you must:

choices.text: Sound the horn to warn cyclists
Open the door quickly to be visible
Check the mirror and look over your shoulder for approaching cyclists
Switch on the hazard lights

explanation.short: Always check mirror and blind spot before opening the door.
explanation.examTip: The driver who opens the door is liable for any collision.

stem: How close to an intersection may you park where parking is not separately signed?

choices.text: Up to the kerb of the intersection
Not within 5 metres of the intersection
Not within 50 metres of the intersection
Anywhere as long as you do not block traffic

explanation.short: Parking is forbidden within 5 metres of an intersection so other road users can see across it.
explanation.examTip: The same 5-metre rule applies near pedestrian crossings and bus stops.

stem: A broken yellow line is painted along the kerb. What does it mean?

choices.text: No stopping, even briefly
No parking, but stopping (to drop off or pick up) is allowed
Loading bay for trucks only
Reserved for taxis

explanation.short: Broken yellow line = no parking; a continuous yellow line = no stopping at all.
explanation.examTip: Remember: continuous = stricter.

stem: On a two-way road, you should park your car:

choices.text: On either side, in either direction
On the right kerb, facing the direction of travel
On the left kerb, facing oncoming traffic
Diagonally across the kerb to save space

explanation.short: Park on the right-hand side facing the direction you were driving, parallel to the kerb.
explanation.examTip: Parking against the flow is allowed only in one-way streets where signed.

stem: May you park briefly in a disabled bay to load shopping if you do not have a disabled permit?

choices.text: Yes, for up to 5 minutes
Yes, if no disabled driver is around
No — disabled bays are reserved at all times for permit holders
Only after 19:00

explanation.short: Disabled parking bays are reserved 24/7 for vehicles displaying a valid disabled parking permit.
explanation.examTip: Fines for misuse are high and the vehicle can be towed.

stem: You are dropping your child at school. The kerb outside has yellow zigzag markings and you would only stop for ten seconds. Is this allowed?

choices.text: Yes — brief stops on zigzags are allowed
Yes — anywhere is allowed for under one minute
No — yellow school zigzags forbid even brief stopping during school hours
Only if you keep the engine running

explanation.short: School zigzag markings prohibit stopping during school hours — park elsewhere and walk the last metres.
explanation.examTip: Sight-lines for crossing children depend on these markings being clear.

image.alt: Yellow zigzag markings along the kerb outside a school where stopping is forbidden during school hours, even for a brief drop-off.
image.caption: School zigzags — no stopping during school hours

stem: You arrive at a blue zone bay at 14:08 with a 60-minute maximum. To what time must you set your parking disc?

choices.text: 14:00, the previous hour
14:08, your actual arrival time
14:30, the next half-hour after arrival
15:08, one hour after arrival

explanation.short: In a blue zone, set the disc to the next half-hour after you actually arrive — 14:30 in this case.
explanation.examTip: You must leave by 15:30; resetting the disc without moving the car is a fine.

stem: You drive an electric car. You finished charging at a public charging bay 30 minutes ago and the bay is busy at this hour. What is the correct action?

choices.text: Stay parked — the bay is yours until you leave
Move the car to a normal parking bay so others can charge
Restart the charging session for a few minutes to reset the timer
Block the bay with a barrier to reserve it

explanation.short: EV charging bays are for vehicles that are actively charging — once full, move on.
explanation.examTip: Many cities apply an idle fee or fine for blocking a charger after charging has ended.

stem: You have parked at the kerb in a busy city street. Before opening the driver's door, what should you do?

choices.text: Open quickly to avoid blocking traffic
Check the mirror and look over the shoulder for cyclists, then open with the far hand (Dutch reach)
Sound the horn first
Open the door wide so cyclists see it from a distance

explanation.short: The Dutch reach forces a shoulder twist that helps you see an approaching cyclist before opening.
explanation.examTip: The driver who opens the door is liable for any collision with a passing cyclist.

image.alt: A car parked at the kerb; before opening the driver door the driver checks the mirror and looks over the shoulder for an approaching cyclist.
image.caption: Look back for cyclists before opening the door (Dutch reach)

stem: You want to drop a passenger right before a zebra crossing. How close to a pedestrian crossing may you stop?

choices.text: Immediately at the crossing, as long as you keep the engine running
Not within 5 metres in front of the crossing — pedestrians must be able to see and be seen
Anywhere, as long as you do not block other cars
Only on a Sunday

explanation.short: Stopping or parking within 5 metres of a zebra crossing is forbidden — it hides pedestrians from approaching drivers.
explanation.examTip: Park or stop 5 m before, never on, a zebra: a child stepping out from behind a stopped car is a classic CBR scenario.

stem: You arrive at a blue-zone bay at 11:47 with a 2-hour maximum, planning to stay 1 hour. To what time must you set your parking disc?

choices.text: 11:47 — the exact arrival time
12:00 — the next full or half hour after arrival
12:47 — one hour later, the planned departure
13:47 — the maximum allowed end time

explanation.short: In Dutch blue zones, the parking disc must be set to the next full or half hour following your actual arrival time.
explanation.examTip: Even if you intend to leave earlier, the disc must always be rounded forward — never backward.

stem: Your electric car has finished charging in a public bay marked 'opladen'. You are eating lunch nearby and the bay is the only one free for waiting EVs. What must you do?

choices.text: Stay parked until you finish lunch — you paid for the bay
Move the car as soon as charging has finished, so another EV driver can use the bay
Leave the car overnight to be safe
Only move it if another EV driver complains

explanation.short: Bays marked 'opladen' are for active charging only. Once charging is complete, you must move the car so the bay stays available.
explanation.examTip: Many Dutch municipalities will fine drivers who occupy a charging bay without actively charging.

stem: You park on a one-way street with cars on both sides. How should your car be facing?

choices.text: Always with traffic flow
Always against traffic flow
Either direction is allowed on a one-way street
Whichever side gives the better space, but always against traffic flow

explanation.short: On a one-way street you may park facing either direction, because there is no oncoming traffic to confuse.
explanation.examTip: On two-way streets you must always park in the direction of travel — only one-way streets allow either direction.

stem: You see a continuous yellow line painted on the kerb of a road. What does this marking mean?

choices.text: Loading and unloading is prohibited here
Stopping and parking are prohibited for all vehicles at all times
Parking is prohibited, but a brief stop to drop someone off is permitted
Parking is restricted to residents only

explanation.short: In the Netherlands, a continuous yellow line on the kerb means stopping and parking are both prohibited at all times.
explanation.examTip: Yellow kerb line = no stopping at all, around the clock. It is a stronger prohibition than a no-parking sign, which allows brief stops.

stem: You want to park near a tram stop. The nearest tram stop sign is 10 metres away. Is this allowed?

choices.text: Yes — 10 metres is a sufficient distance from a tram stop
No — you must not park within 12 metres of a tram or bus stop sign
Yes — distance rules only apply to bus stops, not tram stops
Yes, if you display a valid parking disc

explanation.short: You must not park within 12 metres of a tram or bus stop sign. At 10 metres, you are inside the prohibited zone.
explanation.examTip: The 12-metre rule ensures passengers can board and alight safely without obstruction. It applies to both tram and bus stops.

stem: You want to park on a narrow two-way street. After parking, only 2.5 metres of road remain for other traffic. May you park here?

choices.text: Yes — 2.5 metres is enough for a single car to pass
No — parking is forbidden if less than 3 metres of road remains free for traffic
Yes, but only if you turn your hazard lights on
Yes, as long as a fire truck can still pass

explanation.short: Dutch law requires at least 3 metres of road to remain free for traffic when you park. Leaving only 2.5 metres means you are obstructing the road.
explanation.examTip: 3 metres is the minimum clearance for one lane of traffic. Emergency vehicles need even more — do not park where you create an obstruction.

stem: You want to park your car 4 metres from the corner of a junction. Is this allowed?

choices.text: Yes — there is no minimum distance rule for parking near junctions
Yes — only bus stops and pedestrian crossings have distance restrictions
No — you must not park within 5 metres of a junction corner
Only if no no-parking signs are visible near the junction

explanation.short: Parking is forbidden within 5 metres of a junction corner. At 4 metres you are inside this prohibited zone — parking here obstructs sight lines for all road users.
explanation.examTip: The 5-metre junction rule protects visibility for drivers approaching from any direction. It applies automatically, even without a sign.

stem: A parking space is marked 'laden en lossen' (loading and unloading). You want to park your private car here for 30 minutes to go shopping. Is this allowed?

choices.text: Yes — 30 minutes is short enough for a loading zone
Yes, if no goods vehicle is waiting to use the space
No — loading zones are only for the active transfer of goods, not for general parking
Only if you display a parking disc showing your arrival time

explanation.short: A 'laden en lossen' bay is reserved for the active loading or unloading of goods. Using it to go shopping — even briefly — is not a permitted use.
explanation.examTip: If you are actively transferring packages or goods between your vehicle and a building, you may use the bay. Shopping on foot does not count as loading.

stem: You park facing downhill with a kerb on your right side. Which way should you turn your front wheels before getting out?

choices.text: Left — away from the kerb — so the car turns away from traffic if it rolls
Right — into the kerb — so the front wheel catches the kerb if the car rolls forward
Straight ahead — turning them makes the steering unnecessarily heavy
It does not matter as long as the handbrake is fully applied

explanation.short: Facing downhill with a kerb to the right: turn wheels right (toward the kerb). If the car rolls forward, the right-front wheel catches the kerb and stops the vehicle.
explanation.examTip: Simple rule — downhill with kerb: wheels INTO the kerb. Uphill with kerb: wheels AWAY from the kerb. No kerb: wheels toward the road edge in both cases.

image.alt: A car parked facing downhill with a kerb on the right and its front wheels turned right, toward the kerb.
image.caption: Downhill with a kerb: turn the front wheels into the kerb

stem: Your car breaks down and you must stop on the motorway hard shoulder. What is the most important first action after stopping?

choices.text: Phone your breakdown service and wait inside the car for help
Switch on hazard lights immediately, then get all occupants out of the car and behind the crash barrier
Stand behind the car to wave traffic past
Place the warning triangle 1 metre behind the car

explanation.short: Hazard lights first, then get everyone out of the car and behind the safety barrier as quickly as possible. The hard shoulder is extremely dangerous — a drifting vehicle can hit a stationary car in seconds.
explanation.examTip: Hard shoulder priority order: hazard lights → occupants behind barrier → warning triangle (at least 50 m back) → phone for help. Never stand between the car and moving traffic.

stem: You want to drop a passenger directly in front of a marked bus stop bay. No bus is currently present. Is a very brief stop of 30 seconds permitted?

choices.text: Yes — dropping someone off is not parking, so it is allowed anywhere
Yes, but only outside bus operating hours
No — stopping in a marked bus stop bay is prohibited for non-buses regardless of duration
Only if another person stays in the car to move it immediately if a bus approaches

explanation.short: A marked bus stop bay is a no-stopping zone for all non-bus vehicles at all times. Even a 30-second drop-off is not permitted — stop elsewhere.
explanation.examTip: The distinction between stopping and parking matters in many places, but not at bus stop bays — both are forbidden there.
